caption: Histopathology from a skin biopsy of a 35-year-old woman presenting with a pigmented patch on the right breast, demonstrating epidermal acanthosis and hyperpigmentation of basal cells, particularly pronounced at the rete pegs of the epidermis. The biopsy findings also included hyperpigmentation of the basement membrane associated with numerous melanophages, with no cytonuclear atypia or melanocytic hyperplasia, which helped to exclude cutaneous melanoma and pigmented Paget's disease. This image supports the retained diagnosis of MAN. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification.

Histopathology demonstrating a heavily pigmented neoplasm, excised from a right retro-mastoid craniotomy. The image shows the neoplasm with cytological features obscured by dense melanin pigment, a common challenge in microscopic examination of such lesions. This heavy pigmentation also complicated the interpretation of immunohistochemistry markers during diagnosis. Based on histological features and location, differential diagnoses considered included melanocytoma of intermediate grade, primary meningeal malignant melanoma, and malignant melanotic nerve sheath tumor. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E), original magnification x100 (low power).

Histopathology stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, showing a heavily pigmented neoplasm. The tumor cells exhibit prominent nuclear pleomorphism, characterized by large, irregular, variable vesicular, and hyperchromatic nuclei with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli. In less pigmented areas, epithelioid cells are arranged in sheets and vague lobules within a focally fibro-collagenous background. The differential diagnosis for this neoplasm includes melanocytoma of intermediate grade, primary meningeal malignant melanoma, and malignant melanotic nerve sheath tumor.

Photomicrograph of a hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained section from an incisional biopsy of an oral lesion in the upper labial mucosa, performed to rule out melanocytic hyperplasia or melanoma. At 10X magnification, the specimen reveals the presence of stratified squamous epithelium backed by fibrous connective tissue stroma. The juxta-epithelial region shows diffuse melanin pigmentation along with the presence of melanocytes and melanophages (macrophages that engulf melanin pigment). Multiple nests of melanin pigment with epithelial cells are appreciated within the epithelium. No evidence of melanocytic hyperplasia or dysplasia was observed, supporting a final diagnosis of oral melanotic macule.

Photomicrograph of a hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained section from an incisional biopsy of an oral lesion in the upper labial mucosa, performed to rule out melanocytic hyperplasia or melanoma. At 40X magnification, the specimen provides a higher-magnification view, revealing the presence of stratified squamous epithelium backed by fibrous connective tissue stroma. The juxta-epithelial region shows diffuse melanin pigmentation along with the presence of melanocytes and melanophages (macrophages that engulf melanin pigment). Multiple nests of melanin pigment with epithelial cells are appreciated within the epithelium. No evidence of melanocytic hyperplasia or dysplasia was observed, supporting a final diagnosis of oral melanotic macule.

Histopathology showing large, pleomorphic (polygonal) tumour cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei, often with prominent nucleoli. Occasional multinucleated cells and scattered pigmented cells are noted, which raised the suspicion of melanoma with rhabdoid features after initial consideration of differential diagnoses such as alveolar soft part sarcoma and rhabdomyosarcoma. Image taken with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) stain at 40× magnification.

Histopathology showing a symmetrical, well-circumscribed papule with a fibro-mucinous stroma containing spindle-shaped and dendritic melanocytes, which sometimes were arranged in whorls. Melanophages are present. These features are consistent with the diagnosis of a sclerosing mucinous blue naevus and help exclude malignant melanoma. Magnification 20x, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain.

Histopathology showing a subepithelial lesion, extending up to the subcutis, composed of sheets and nests of cells arranged in a zellballen pattern. The cells feature clear to eosinophilic cytoplasm and vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli. This pattern is suggestive of paraganglioma. No epidermal involvement, pagetoid spread, atypia, increased mitotic activity, or necrosis were observed, helping to rule out malignant tumors like melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×10 magnification.

Photomicrograph showing multiple (at least nine) distinct nests of glandular epithelial structures within the capsular tissue of a postneoadjuvant, ipsilateral, and axillary sentinel lymph node. Some of these epithelial nests abutted the subcapsular sinus. These nodal capsular glandular cell groups were discontinuous and encircled approximately one-half of the circumference of the 3.5 mm lymph node. The histologic differential diagnosis for these glandular structures within the lymph node included metastatic disease versus benign/reactive heterotopic epithelium (Hematoxylin and Eosin [H&E] stain, original magnification 400x).

Histopathology (H&E, ×40) from a punch biopsy of a rapidly enlarging nodule in a congenital melanocytic nevus shows cellular proliferation of melanocytes with no deep infiltration. This finding is observed in a 28-year-old pregnant woman presenting with rapidly enlarging papules and nodules in a 15 cm-sized black-pigmented plaque on her right posterior thigh. The biopsy was performed to exclude malignant melanoma arising within the congenital melanocytic nevus. The final diagnosis was benign proliferative nodules with mild atypia arising in a congenital melanocytic nevus.

Histopathology (H&E, ×100) from a punch biopsy of a rapidly enlarging nodule in a congenital melanocytic nevus shows proliferation of melanocytes in the background of a melanocytic nevus. This finding is observed in a 28-year-old pregnant woman presenting with rapidly enlarging papules and nodules in a 15 cm-sized black-pigmented plaque on her right posterior thigh. The biopsy was performed to exclude malignant melanoma arising within the congenital melanocytic nevus. The final diagnosis was benign proliferative nodules with mild atypia arising in a congenital melanocytic nevus.

Histopathology (H&E, ×200) from a punch biopsy of a rapidly enlarging nodule in a congenital melanocytic nevus shows intraepidermal pagetoid proliferation of melanocytes. The distribution of these pagetoid cells was mostly confined to the lower part of the epidermis, without mitosis, necrosis, and high-grade atypia. This finding is observed in a 28-year-old pregnant woman presenting with rapidly enlarging papules and nodules in a 15 cm-sized black-pigmented plaque on her right posterior thigh. The biopsy was performed to exclude malignant melanoma arising within the congenital melanocytic nevus. The final diagnosis was benign proliferative nodules with mild atypia arising in a congenital melanocytic nevus.

Histology of hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC)-associated renal cell carcinoma (RCC) from a 57-year-old man, diagnosed by genetic testing. HLRCC is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a germline mutation in fumarate hydratase (FH), an enzyme involved in the tricarboxylic acid cycle. The image shows oncocytic tumor cells arranged in a solid sheet-like pattern. This rare, low-grade oncocytic morphology in HLRCC-associated RCC can be reminiscent of SDH-deficient RCC. The tumor cells were indistinguishable from oncocytomas by H&E staining alone, highlighting the challenges in morphological differential diagnosis.

Histopathological image from an incisional biopsy of the buccal mucosa, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 100x magnification. The image shows parakeratosis and acanthosis of the stratified squamous epithelium, with melanin confined to the basal layer. Clusters of melanophages are visible within the connective tissue papilla, indicative of phagocytosed melanin within macrophages in the upper connective tissue. Foci of lymphocytic infiltrate are present in the basal layer, associated with spongiosis and occasional cytoid bodies. There is no evidence of dysplasia or malignancy. These findings, correlating with clinical observations, contributed to the diagnosis of Laugier-Hunziker syndrome, effectively excluding melanoma from the differential diagnosis.

Photomicrograph (H&E, 20x magnification) from an excisional biopsy, illustrating the histopathological findings of a stromal lesion. The image shows numerous large foamy cells, also known as xanthoma cells or foamy macrophages, characterized by pale cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei, located within the connective tissue stroma. These findings are part of a broader histopathological evaluation which also revealed stratified squamous epithelium of variable thickness overlying the connective tissue stroma with ulceration, and a mild to moderate chronic inflammatory cell infiltration predominantly lymphocytes and plasma cells. This biopsy was performed following a provisional clinical diagnosis of mucocele, with traumatic fibroma considered in the differential diagnosis.

Histopathology of an ocular tumor showing differentiation. Differentiation is a pathological tissue image feature used to distinguish between benign and malignant ocular tumors. This feature was identified as an independent influencing factor in the diagnostic and differential diagnosis of ocular masses, with its presence or absence contributing to the characterization of the tumor's property. Image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 200x magnification.

Histopathology of an ocular tumor showing structural atypia. Structural atypia is a pathological tissue image feature that helps differentiate between benign and malignant ocular tumors. This feature, characterized by organized or disorderly growth, was identified as an independent influencing factor in the diagnostic and differential diagnosis of ocular masses. Image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 100x magnification.

Histopathology of an ocular tumor showing covering epithelium. The presence or absence of covering epithelium is a pathological tissue image feature used to distinguish between benign and malignant ocular tumors. This feature was identified as an independent influencing factor in the diagnostic and differential diagnosis of ocular masses. Image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 40x magnification.

Histopathology of an ocular tumor showing nuclear atypia. Nuclear atypia is a significant pathological tissue image feature used for differentiating between benign and malignant ocular tumors. This characteristic was identified as an independent influencing factor in the diagnostic and differential diagnosis of ocular masses. Image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 100x magnification.

Histopathology of an ocular tumor showing nuclear division. Nuclear division, indicative of mitotic activity, is a critical pathological tissue image feature for differentiating between benign and malignant ocular tumors. This characteristic was identified as an independent influencing factor in the diagnostic and differential diagnosis of ocular masses. Image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 200x magnification.
Hematoxylin and eosin image following treatment showing no evidence of residual melanoma in situ.

Low-magnification histopathology (25x) of the lung lesion, found in a 36-year-old female patient 16 years after her primary cutaneous melanoma. The lung mass was initially thought to be a non-small cell lesion (possibly squamous cell carcinoma) based on fine needle aspiration, with negative melanoma markers (HMB45, MART) and weakly positive p63. Evaluation of the pneumonectomy specimen, however, led to a differential diagnosis with metastatic melanoma, as some features were consistent with melanocytic differentiation (weakly positive Vimentin, tyrosinase, and mitf). This image shows a nodular proliferation of atypical, partly plasmacytoid cells resembling the primary cutaneous melanoma. (Hematoxylin and eosin stain)

High-magnification histopathology (100x) of the lung lesion, found in a 36-year-old female patient 16 years after her primary cutaneous melanoma. The lung mass was initially thought to be a non-small cell lesion (possibly squamous cell carcinoma) based on fine needle aspiration, with negative melanoma markers (HMB45, MART) and weakly positive p63. Evaluation of the pneumonectomy specimen, however, led to a differential diagnosis with metastatic melanoma, as some features were consistent with melanocytic differentiation (weakly positive Vimentin, tyrosinase, and mitf). This image shows a nodular proliferation of atypical, partly plasmacytoid cells resembling the primary cutaneous melanoma. (Hematoxylin and eosin stain)

High-magnification histopathology (100x) of the lung lesion, found in a 36-year-old female patient 16 years after her primary cutaneous melanoma. The lung mass was initially thought to be a non-small cell lesion (possibly squamous cell carcinoma) based on fine needle aspiration, with negative melanoma markers (HMB45, MART) and weakly positive p63.
